coredata.dc:title: Fluorimetric detection of distinct lyotropic anion interactions on nanoscopic surfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Rana, Shameema
Kherb, Jaibir
coredata.dc:description: The molecular structure, stability and function of proteins, nucleic acids, and many other colloidal entities including nanomaterials depend greatly on their highly specific response towards surrounding solvent molecules and inorganic ions. In order to earn an unambiguous mechanistic insight into the origin of these effects, water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanomaterials having amide groups in a hydrophobic microenvironment were successfully employed as the model colloidal system in this research study. Quantum dots with acidic functionalities were first of all prepared using a simple carbon precursor. Subsequently, chemical modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was carried out with isopropylamine to impart protein-like amide richness to the nanoparticle surface. Moreover, the prepared CQDs were found to be highly fluorescent. Fluorescence intensity, interestingly, showed unique modulation patterns on incubation with different types of anions. Lipophilic chaotropic anions such as I−, SCN− , and Br− showed concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching whereas hydrophilic kosmotropic anions like SO4 2− had almost no impact on the nanomaterial fluorescence. This striking observation in the case of chaotropic anions can be correlated to their strong partitioning affinity for the amide groups on the nanomaterial surface. High charge density anions like sulphate, on the other hand, are mostly excluded from the colloidal interfaces. The current study emphasizes the fact that the functional groups available on the surface of macromolecules as well as anion's inherent thermodynamic parameters in a solvent play a pivotal role in the observed anion specificity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lyotropic series
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Ionic partitioning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence measurements of the functionalized CQDs with anions
Morphological and structural features of the functionalized CQDs
Electronic characteristics of the functionalized CQDs
Fluorescence studies of the interaction of anions with NCQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Material Science and Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur (HP), India for the financial support in carrying out this research. The support of the Department of Material Science and Engineering (DMSE) at the National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur (HP) is also highly acknowledged for providing the facilities for sample characterization throughout the research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) high-resolution TEM image (inset: SAED pattern), (c) DLS plot, and (d) wide-range XRD pattern of synthesized NCQDs.
(a) XPS survey scan, (b) the high-resolution C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s spectrum of NCQDs.
(a) UV–visible spectrum showing various absorption bands and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs at varying excitation wavelengths in 10 nm increments.
Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs in the presence of (a) sodium iodide, (b) sodium chloride, (c) sodium sulphate concentrations, and (d) 50 mM concentration of various anions.
Partitioning behavior of anions at NCQD-water interface.
Plot of fluorescence intensity change (f) of NCQDs on incubation with an increasing amount of various lyotropic salts.
Correlation of Kd values to (a) polarizabilities, (b) free energies of hydration, and (c) hydrated radii of chaotropic anions.
Correlation of Kd values to (a) surface tension increments, and (b) entropies of hydration for kosmotropic anions.
Proposed reaction scheme for the formation of amide-functionalized CQDs.
Values of binding parameters obtained from the fitting of data applying Eq. (1).
Literature values of various thermodynamic properties of the tested anions.

head.ce:title: Fluorimetric detection of distinct lyotropic anion interactions on nanoscopic surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur, Himachal Pradesh 177005, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur, Himachal Pradesh – 177005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The molecular structure, stability and function of proteins, nucleic acids, and many other colloidal entities including nanomaterials depend greatly on their highly specific response towards surrounding solvent molecules and inorganic ions. In order to earn an unambiguous mechanistic insight into the origin of these effects, water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanomaterials having amide groups in a hydrophobic microenvironment were successfully employed as the model colloidal system in this research study. Quantum dots with acidic functionalities were first of all prepared using a simple carbon precursor. Subsequently, chemical modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was carried out with isopropylamine to impart protein-like amide richness to the nanoparticle surface. Moreover, the prepared CQDs were found to be highly fluorescent. Fluorescence intensity, interestingly, showed unique modulation patterns on incubation with different types of anions. Lipophilic chaotropic anions such as I−, SCN− , and Br− showed concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching whereas hydrophilic kosmotropic anions like SO4 2− had almost no impact on the nanomaterial fluorescence. This striking observation in the case of chaotropic anions can be correlated to their strong partitioning affinity for the amide groups on the nanomaterial surface. High charge density anions like sulphate, on the other hand, are mostly excluded from the colloidal interfaces. The current study emphasizes the fact that the functional groups available on the surface of macromolecules as well as anion's inherent thermodynamic parameters in a solvent play a pivotal role in the observed anion specificity.

ce:section.ce:para: In aqueous solutions, salts have a significant impact on the three-dimensional structure, aggregation behavior as well as the function of different types of solubilized macromolecules like proteins, ion channels, nucleic acids, surfactants, and nanomaterials [1]. Very unique specificity among different anions was first reported by Franz Hofmeister in 1888 in his research work involving serum albumin proteins. An arrangement of ions, namely Hofmeister series, signifying their strength to salt-out proteins had been provided. For anions, the series is typically reported as:

The series classifies anions into significantly hydrated ‘kosmotropes’ and imperfectly hydrated ‘chaotropes’. Kosmotropic anions, lying on the left-hand side of chloride ion, cause salting-out of proteins whereas chaotropic anions lying on the right of Cl− lead to salting-in of proteins [2,3]. It has been speculated that these ions dramatically alters the structure of water. Kosmotropes, being capable of organizing plenty of water molecules around them, pulls water molecules away from proteins resulting in the salting-out phenomenon. Contrarily, chaotropes are unable to organize molecules of water around them and result in salting-in behavior by lending water molecules to the proteins [4]. The chloride ion, which appears to be the dividing point between two distinct groups of ions, has insignificant effects on the water structure [5]. Unlike anions, the specific cation effects are generally weaker. This is because of relatively firmer anion-water interactions than those of cations with water [6]. The ranking for cations series follows the trend:

In contrast with anions, it is the poorly hydrated chaotropic cations lying on the left of sodium ion which show salting-out behavior whereas heavily hydrated kosmotropic cations lying on the right exhibit salting-in behavior [7]. Numerous physiological phenomena, apart from protein folding and function, have been identified over the last century where these Hofmeister salt effects are directly involved, be it polymer stability, wetting of surfaces, and micelle formation [3,8]. For decades, it was wrongly presumed that the ion interactions influence the behavior of macromolecular systems by simply altering the bulk water structure [9]. This explanation, however, has been disproved by various research studies and alternative approaches [1,10–12]. It has been found that these ionic effects can be viewed as a manifestation of their direct interactions with the macromolecule [8,13]. However, a detailed molecular-level understanding of these anionic effects and their correlation to macromolecule/ion properties such as size, hydrophobicity, and polarity is still lacking [10,14]. Simple experimental model systems with strong surface-specific spectral signals must be systematically tested to explore the underlying mechanism.

In the area of nanotechnology, tremendous progress has occurred over the last few decades resulting in the synthesis of diverse types of nanomaterials and recognition of their vast physiochemical/biological applications [15–17]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have stood out as a fascinating member among the nanocarbon family. CQDs are a part of zero-dimensional structures comprising quasi-spheric graphitic nanoparticles with dimensions, not above 10 nm [18,19]. In general, carbon and materials based on carbon are mostly regarded as black, insoluble materials which show weak fluorescence [20]. However, CQDs have aroused a keen research interest given their strong fluorescence and high solubility in water. When compared with the semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs are much superior vis-a-vis their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high resistance to photobleaching, facile functionalization, easily available raw materials and economical, simple methods of synthesis [21,22]. These merits make them ideal candidates for applications in bioimaging [23,24], sensing [25], drug delivery [26], and catalysis [27]. The occurrence of various functional groups at the CQD surface relies on the precursors used for their synthesis. Functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amine not only provide hydrophilicity but also ensure further opportunities for surface functionalization [28]. Due to these inherent functional groups or further ligation attachments, several defects may also occur on the surface of the CQDs. Such defects serve as surface energy traps responsible for the changes in fluorescence properties of the CQDs [29]. The functionalization of the CQDs can be attained through either of two types of linkages: covalent or non-covalent linkage. Covalent linkages occur when the functionalizing agent reacts with the groups, such as carboxyl, present on the CQD surface. However, non-covalent linkages involve the van der Waals interactions or π interactions between the CQDs and the functionalizing agent [28]. In brief, the functionalization of CQDs leads to an improvement in their stability, biocompatibility, and optical properties [30]. These spectrally sensitive nanomaterials with good water solubility and controllable surface chemistry are an ideal model system to investigate specific salt interactions that occur on macromolecule-solvent interfaces [31].

In this research study, we have explored the fluorescent CQDs with amide surface functionality in a hydrophobic microenvironment for the detailed assessment of specific anion interactions. In a way, this system resembles the fundamental structure of proteins which contains amide group in the backbone with predominantly hydrophobic amino acids in the near vicinity. Firstly, CQDs with carboxylic acid functionality were generated using the thermal decomposition of a simple carbon precursor, malonic acid. Subsequently, CQDs were treated with isopropylamine solution which resulted in the generation of amide functionalities on the nanomaterial surface. The isopropyl group connected to the amide provides a unique testing platform for checking the partitioning or binding affinity of any anions to the protein backbones in aqueous solutions. Amide functionalized CQDs, when incubated with varying concentrations of different anions, exhibited striking variations in their fluorescence behavior. The specificity of interaction of anions with the functionalities on the CQDs varied greatly from one ion to another. In summary, we have successfully evaluated the mechanism of anionic interactions on macromolecules using a fluorescent probe having precisely designed amide functionality on the molecular surface.

Analytical grade samples of malonic acid, isopropylamine, glycine, and all the sodium salts having purity ≥99% were bought from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. All the salt solutions and buffer solutions were prepared in freshly obtained nanopure water collected in the laboratory.

The absorption studies on aqueous samples were carried out at room temperature on a T80 + ™ UV–visible spectrophotometer (PG Instruments Ltd). The nanomaterial solution was centrifuged by a REMI C-30BL instrument (REMI Sales and Engineering Ltd.), followed by ultra-sonication using a PCI Analytics ultrasonic bath. Fluorescence analysis of solutions was conducted on a Shimadzu spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC model). The particle size of the synthesized nanomaterial sample and its morphological features were visualized using a JSM-6610 scanning electron microscope and a high-resolution JEOL JEM-2100 Plus electron microscope. Zeta potential measurements were performed at room temperature on a Malvern Nano-ZS series zeta sizer instrument. The nanomaterial sample was dried for the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis for which Rigaku Smart Lab diffractometer having Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54 Å) was employed. Surface states were explored using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Prevac) and functional group analysis was carried with Perkin Elmer L1600312 FTIR spectrometer in the wavenumber range 4000–400 cm−1.

Firstly, CQDs were obtained by carbonizing an adequate amount of malonic acid using silica crucible in the muffle furnace. The heating was carried out at 400 °C for 5 min, which yielded CQDs in powdered form. Subsequently, CQDs were dissolved in 35 ml volume of deionized water. 10 ml of pure isopropylamine was then added to the obtained colloidal solution. Thereafter, the solution was refluxed at 180 °C for about 24 h. A brownish-yellow colored solution was obtained, which was further ultrasonicated and then centrifuged at a speed of 6000 rpm for 30 min to isolate the larger nanoparticles from the CQDs aqueous solution. After filtering the solution, CQDs were oven-dried at 80 °C for two hours. The dried CQDs were redissolved in 10 mM glycine-HCl buffer (pH 2.2) to obtain a transparent and clear solution of amide-functionalized CQDs (say, NCQDs). This stock solution of NCQDs was utilized for all of the further fluorescence experiments in the presence of different types of sodium salts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurements of the functionalized CQDs with anions
ce:section.ce:para: The specific interactions of the anions with the NCQDs were investigated using fluorescence spectroscopic studies. For this purpose, stock solutions of all the sodium salts of known concentrations were prepared in 10 mM glycine-HCl buffer having pH 2.2. Different concentrations of the salts were obtained from these stock solutions using the buffer solution for dilution.

Initially, a detailed fluorescence emission response of NCQDs was recorded in the absence of any salt. The obtained spectra exhibited a sharp emission peak at 423 nm when the NCQDs solution was irradiated using 370 nm excitation wavelength. The magnitude of this fluorescence emission was taken as the initial intensity. Thereafter, the fluorescence of NCQDs was measured after incubating them with varying concentrations of different Hofmeister anions. For this purpose, the NCQDs solution was mixed with the salt solution in a ratio of 1:1 to attain the desired concentration. The resultant mixture was allowed to be incubated for 5 min, and then its fluorescence response was measured at the specified excitation wavelength. The given procedure was replicated at varying concentrations of all the salt solutions to monitor their impact on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized NCQDs. Overall, fluorescence data of NCQDs in the presence of increasing amounts of Na2SO4, NaH2PO4, NaF, NaCl, NaBr, NaI, and NaSCN salts were collected and exhaustively studied. Each of the fluorescence measurements reported in the study has been repeated three times to ascertain reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural features of the functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Size and morphological details of the prepared NCQDs were verified using transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images. As clearly reflected in Fig. 1a, the NCQDs were mainly spherical in shape with good monodispersity. Prepared NCQDs manifest a narrow size distribution with a mean particle size of 4 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) displayed two types of crystal lattices showing visible lattice fringes with 0.22 nm and 0.35 nm spacing, which denote the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphite and inter-planar spacing of graphite respectively [32]. Moreover, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern displayed several small spots making up concentric rings, which confirm the polycrystalline character of NCQDs [33]. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, shown in Fig. 1c, of water suspended NCQDs revealed an average hydrodynamic size of around 54 nm. The agglomeration behavior of NCQDs in the aqueous medium might have been responsible for the larger size estimated from DLS [34,35]. The degree of crystallinity of NCQDs was further explored by XRD. The XRD spectra of synthesized NCQDs showed a slightly broad diffraction peak at around 250 with two shoulder peaks at approximately 430 and 600, as shown in Fig. 1d. These peaks are indexed to the (002), (100), and (004) graphitic planes, respectively [36]. Using the Bragg equation, i.e., nλ = 2dsinθ, the d-spacing was found to be 0.38 nm and 0.21 nm which corresponds to diffraction peaks of (002) and (100) planes, respectively. These d-spacing values correlate well with the values derived from the HRTEM results. The observed interlayer distance of 0.38 nm in NCQDs is slightly larger in magnitude as compared to that of the bulk graphite (0.33 nm). This may be ascribed to the introduction of isopropyl adjoining amide moieties at the NCQD surface, which enhances the spacing between the layers and also imparts a little amorphous character to the CQDs [36].

FTIR spectrum of the prepared NCQDs was examined to confirm the presence of amide functionality on the nanomaterial surface (Fig. 2 ). An absorption band in the wavenumber range at 3400–3200 cm−1 can be ascribed to –OH and –NH stretching vibrations [37]. The vibrational fingerprints at 1649 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1 are usually accredited to the amide –C=O stretching and secondary –N-H bending as well as –C=C stretching, respectively [38–40]. A small but significant peak at 1257 cm−1 arises due to –C-N bond stretching [40]. The absorption peaks at 2927 cm−1, 1409 cm−1, 1354 cm−1 and 743 cm−1, originate from the asymmetric -C-H stretching, asymmetric -C-H bend, symmetric -C-H bend, and aromatic –C-H out-of-plane bending, respectively. The peak at 1050 cm−1 is due to –C-O stretching vibrations [40]. The appearance of –C=O, -N-H, -C-N, and –C-H signature bands in the FTIR spectrum indicated successful incorporation of isopropyl adjoining amide functionalization of the CQDs.

Formation of amide bonds on the nanoparticle surface proceeds via facile intermolecular condensation reaction as shown in Scheme 1 . The reaction involves simple condensation of amine groups of isopropylamine substrate with the carboxyl functionality on CQDs resulting in the formation of amide moiety and loss of water molecules as side-product [41].

The chemical structure and elemental composition of NCQDs were further explored by using XPS analysis. The XPS survey scan depicted in Fig. 3a exhibited three major peaks at binding energy values of 285.1 eV (C1s), 400.3 eV (N1s), and 532.6 eV (O1s) [42]. This indicates that carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen constitute a great proportion of the NCQDs with their respective elemental contents being 56.1%, 5.3%, and 32.9%. A small amount of sodium was also observed with Na1s peak at 1072 eV, which may be due to sodium diffusion to the sample surface from the glass slide used for the analysis [43].

Fig. 3b presents the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which can be fitted into four characteristic peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.4 eV suggesting the existence of C-C/C=C, C-N-C, CO, and O=C-NH/O=C-OH respectively [44,45]. The deconvolution of the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3c) reveals the presence of two peaks at 400.2 eV and 402 eV, which may be attributed to –C-N and –N-H groups respectively [46,47]. Additionally, the XPS fitting of O1s shows peaks at 530.9 eV, 532.4 eV, and 535 eV, which are ascribed to –C=O, -C-O, and Na KLL, respectively [36,48,49]. Therefore, XPS and FTIR results complement each other well and reflect the successful amide functionalization of the CQDs.

The above findings were also supported by the zeta potential analysis of NCQDs, which was measured as - 0.54 mV i.e. nearly zero. An insignificant negative value of zeta potential further substantiates that most of the carboxylic acid groups have been converted into amide groups, and the synthesized NCQDs contain amide groups on their periphery [50] (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic characteristics of the functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of NCQDs were inspected by collecting light absorption and fluorescence emission data in the UV–vis region. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of NCQDs dispersed in 10 mM glycine-HCl buffer, pH 2.2 is shown in Fig. 5a which exhibits a broad absorption band with two clear shoulders at 230 nm and 282 nm with a comparatively weak absorption shoulder at 339 nm. The 230 nm absorption band can be ascribed to the π-π⁎ transitions of CC bond in the aromatic carbon core, whereas the other two bands are related to the n-π⁎ transitions in CO of amide groups at the NCQD surface [51,52]. To get a deeper insight into their optical response, detailed fluorescence spectra of NCQDs dispersed in 10 mM glycine-HCl buffer (pH = 2.2) were recorded at varying excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5b). The spectra clearly exhibited a highly fluorescent nature of the synthesized NCQDs. The probable cause for their strong fluorescence is the radiative recombination of the excited electrons from n-π⁎ transitions of surface CO and nitrogen moieties [53]. Besides, a gradual change in the intensity and red-shift in the emission maximum was observed with the increasing wavelength of excitation varied from 320 nm to 400 nm in intervals of 10 nm. This highlights the excitation-dependent emission behavior of prepared NCQDs. This behavior has been very commonly reported in CQD research studies and has been associated with the particle-size distribution and different surface states, which act as emissive trap sites. At different excitation wavelengths, corresponding states display their characteristic emission features [54–56]. The most intense fluorescence emission was observed at 423 nm when the NCQDs were excited with 370 nm wavelength. This indicates the excitation of the maximum number of nanoparticles at this particular wavelength [57]. Moreover, few shoulders also appeared in the fluorescence spectra, which can be attributed to the emission from different types of chromophores at slightly different wavelengths [58]. From the above fluorescence spectra analysis, the excitation wavelength resulting in the maximum emission (λex = 370 nm) was chosen to perform all of the further fluorescence studies in the presence of salts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence studies of the interaction of anions with NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selective fluorescence response of NCQDs towards various anions was evaluated in detail. For this purpose, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs dispersed in 10 mM glycine-HCl buffer (pH = 2.2) was recorded against variable concentrations of different inorganic sodium salt solutions.

Fig. 6 represents the effect of some of the chaotropic and kosmotropic anions on the fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs. All of these emission spectra have been recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. As demonstrated in Fig. 6a, the increasing concentration of chaotropic iodide ion to the NCQDs aqueous solution produced a quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs. This observation signifies that the iodide ions partition to the amide interface of NCQDs and induce strong negative surface potential. Fluorescence of CQD samples has been known to respond dramatically to the surface potential [59,60]. The addition of chloride ion, on the contrary, reduced the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs to a much lesser extent as seen in Fig. 6b. Further, as depicted in Fig. 6c, fluorescence emission intensity stayed almost the same on the addition of strongly kosmotropic sulphate ion to the NCQDs solution. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity remains almost unaltered even at very higher concentrations of sulphate anion. Fig. 6d represents the comparative fluorescence data of NCQDs in the presence of 50 mM concentration of NaI, NaSCN, NaBr, NaCl, NaF, NaH2PO4, and Na2SO4 solutions prepared in the glycine-HCl buffer, pH 2.2. The unique specificity of different anions was clearly discernible. It was observed that even a small amount of chaotropic anions such as I−, SCN− and Br− diminish the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs very strongly. On the contrary, Cl− ion exhibits a lesser influence on the intensity whereas there is almost no change in the fluorescence intensity in the case of kosmotropic anions such as F−, H2PO4 −, and SO4 2−.

The molecular-level difference in interactions of chaotropic and kosmotropic ions to macromolecules was clearly evident through these fluorescence modulations of NCQDs. It should be recalled that the NCQD surface contains amide moieties with isopropyl groups in the near vicinity. Chaotropic anions, owing to their hydrophobicity and lesser water propensity, have a greater affinity for the isopropyl adjoining amide moieties on the NCQD surface which allows them to weaken the hydrophobic effect and interact strongly with NCQDs (Fig. 7a) [61]. These ions may directly bind to the amide moieties as well as cause the salting-in effect as displayed in Fig. 7b [62]. On the other hand, kosmotropic anions interact strongly with the molecules of water and thus remain in the bulk solution as displayed in Fig. 7 [63].

The fitting of the fluorescence quenching data of NCQDs obtained after incubation with various anions was performed by using a modified Langmuir binding Eq. (I):
(I)
f =
B max
M
e
M
K d
+
M
e
M

In this equation, f is the difference between the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs before and after the addition of salts. Bmax is a constant, which reflects the maximum change in fluorescence intensity after the addition of salt. [M] represents the molar concentration of the salt and Kd refers to the dissociation constant of anion interaction with the NCQDs. The lower value of Kd signifies tighter interaction between the anion and binding pockets on the nanoparticle surface. The plot of function ‘f’ against increasing molar concentration of salts showed an excellent fit to the above mentioned binding equation as can be seen in Fig. 8 .

The shape of the fitting curves seems an exact imitation of the conventional Langmuir binding isotherm which indicates a saturation binding phenomenon. The curves at the top and the bottom of the plot represent the binding propensity of the chaotropic and the kosmotropic anions respectively. Obtained results comprehensively showed that the change in the emission intensity was exponentially high at low millimolar concentrations of chaotropic salts whereas the difference in the intensities became insignificantly small at very high concentrations. This striking behavior was due to the availability of a finite number of binding sites on the NCQD surface. Once these binding sites get saturated, even the chaotropic anions have a negligible effect on the nanomaterial fluorescence. For all of the chaotropes, a sharp change in the fluorescence intensity occurred up to 200 mM concentration range. On the contrary, no major change in the NCQDs fluorescence was seen for kosmotropic anions in the same concentration regime. Fitting of fluorescence data to Eq. (I) yielded anionic Kd and Bmax values which are summarized in Table 1 .

Quite clearly, the Kd values obtained for the chaotropic anions were much smaller than those for the kosmotropic anions. Furthermore, among the chaotropes, iodide ion had the lowest Kd value implying its strongest binding affinity to the NCQDs surface. Larger values of Kd for the kosmotropic anions indicate their minimal interaction with the NCQDs. Further, Bmax values obtained for chaotropes were much higher which means that those anions tend to cause a greater change in the fluorescence emission of NCQDs. Thus, the ions such as I−, SCN−, and Br− strongly quench the fluorescence. Contrastingly, significantly lower values of Bmax with kosmotropes show a very weak association of these ions to nanomaterial surface. Fluoride ion among kosmotropes showed unexpectedly higher Bmax value which could be a direct consequence of large pH alteration when a high amount of NaF salt was added to the NCQD solution. More specifically, an increase in solution pH after the dissolution of a high amount of sodium fluoride might have caused NCQDs to show some quenching of fluorescence [31,64]. Solution pH was invariable for all other tested salts. Hence, from the binding parameter values, it could be unambiguously stated that the anions partitioned to the amide containing nanomaterial surface in the following order: I− > SCN− > Br− > F− > Cl− > H2PO4 − > SO4 2−.

The anion binding data to NCQDs summarized in Table 1 implies that the chaotropic anions certainly bind more tightly to the nanomaterial surface than the kosmotropic ones. It should be noted that all of the anions beyond Br− bound very weakly to the NCQDs surface. Chloride may be considered as the Hofmeister-neutral ion as it does not show any preferential affinity for the NCQD surface or to the bulk solution. To have a mechanistic understanding of these specific responses induced by added anions, we looked into several possible correlations between calculated binding parameters and the inherent thermodynamic properties of the studied anions. The literature values of hydrated radii (rhyd), polarizabilities (α), free energies of hydration (ΔGhyd), entropies of hydration (ΔShyd), and surface tension increments (σ) of the anions are provided in Table 2 .

Polarizability of an anion plays a vital role in driving its partitioning or exclusion from the molecular surface. The polarizability values for tested chaotropes showed an excellent direct correlation with their Kd values as displayed in Fig. 9a. More polarizable and larger chaotropic anions have a higher binding affinity for the isopropyl proximate amide interface of NCQDs [2]. Thus, the ions with the highest values of polarizability yielded the least values of dissociation constant. In addition, the Kd values for chaotropes expectedly showed a good correlation to their free energy of hydration and the hydrated radii as presented in Fig. 9b and Fig. 9c respectively. Earlier research studies and ionic databases have shown a direct connection between hydration free energy and radii of ions [69]. Significantly larger anions have smaller free energies of hydration. Bigger ions, therefore, can efficiently shed their hydration shells and directly bind to the NCQDs, which is in agreement with the binding parameters obtained from the experimental data.

On the other hand, kosmotropes data did not show any direct correlation with their polarizability, the free energy of hydration, and hydrated radii. Instead, a positive correlation between the surface tension increment values and the calculated Kd values was observed for kosmotropes as demonstrated in Fig. 10a. Kosmotropic anions induce a much larger increase in surface tension of the NCQDs aqueous solution and tend to stay in the bulk, thus resulting in almost no direct binding interactions at the NCQD surface [71]. Furthermore, as can be seen in Fig. 10b, the trend of Kd values and hydration entropy of anions exhibited an inverse correlation in the case of kosmotropes. Presumably, in aqueous solution, water molecules surrounding the NCQDs via hydrogen-bonding with the amide moieties form a hydration shell around the macromolecular surface. This hydration shell gets disrupted by the addition of kosmotropic anions. Therefore, the kosmotropic effects are not due to direct interaction with the macromolecule surface. Their observed effects are a consequence of them bringing structural changes in the water molecules in the immediate vicinity of macromolecules [2,70].

Herein, we have presented a fluorescence-based model probe to investigate the mechanism of anionic lyotropic series. Functionalized CQDs having surface amide groups displayed a dramatic concentration-dependent quenching of fluorescence in the presence of chaotropic anions. Effects of chaotropic and kosmotropic anions on the aqueous suspended molecules proceeds via fundamentally different pathways. The preferable interaction of chaotropes with the NCQDs may be attributed to their substantial direct binding affinity for the amide group on the molecular surface. However, for uncharged colloidal solutions, kosmotropic anions are excluded from the solute surface. Their salting-out impact is due to their strong affinity for water molecules in the immediate vicinity of the macromolecule.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur (HP), India for the financial support in carrying out this research. The support of the Department of Material Science and Engineering (DMSE) at the National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur (HP) is also highly acknowledged for providing the facilities for sample characterization throughout the research work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple theory for the Hofmeister series

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Xie, Y.Q. Gao, A simple theory for the Hofmeister series, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 4 (2013) 4247–4252. doi:10.1021/jz402072g.
